questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: what is a common role of a load balancer?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Health checks can prevent routing to unhealthy instances.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Health checks can prevent routing to unhealthy instances.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Health checks can prevent routing to unhealthy instances.

answers.text: The way I understand it: distribute incoming requests across healthy service instances.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Distribute incoming requests across healthy service instances.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Distribute incoming requests across healthy service instances.

answers.text: Short version: A load balancer routes traffic; it is not the primary database.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A load balancer routes traffic; it is not the primary database.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A load balancer routes traffic; it is not the primary database.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: why is a cache placed in front of a slower data source?

answers.text: The way I understand it: to serve repeated reads with lower latency and less backend load.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. To serve repeated reads with lower latency and less backend load.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. To serve repeated reads with lower latency and less backend load.

answers.text: Short version: To guarantee that data can never become stale.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Caches need an explicit freshness and invalidation strategy.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Caches need an explicit freshness and invalidation strategy.

answers.text: I'd go with this: A cache miss still requires access to the underlying source.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A cache miss still requires access to the underlying source.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A cache miss still requires access to the underlying source.

questions.prompt: Forum check: what does data replication provide?

answers.text: From what I've seen, automatic protection from every application bug.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A bad write can be replicated to every copy.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A bad write can be replicated to every copy.

answers.text: The practical answer is: a live replica is a complete substitute for an independent backup.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Replication can copy deletion or corruption, so recovery still needs an appropriate backup strategy.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Replication can copy deletion or corruption, so recovery still needs an appropriate backup strategy.

answers.text: My answer: Additional copies that can improve availability or read capacity.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Additional copies that can improve availability or read capacity.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Additional copies that can improve availability or read capacity.

questions.prompt: Curious where people land on this: what does database sharding change?

answers.text: My answer: Cross-shard queries and transactions can become more complex.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Cross-shard queries and transactions can become more complex.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Cross-shard queries and transactions can become more complex.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: It partitions data across multiple database nodes.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It partitions data across multiple database nodes.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It partitions data across multiple database nodes.

answers.text: The way I understand it: the partition key determines placement and strongly affects balance.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The partition key determines placement and strongly affects balance.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The partition key determines placement and strongly affects balance.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: why are idempotency keys useful for write APIs?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: They help repeated delivery avoid creating the same logical operation twice.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. They help repeated delivery avoid creating the same logical operation twice.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. They help repeated delivery avoid creating the same logical operation twice.

answers.text: The way I understand it: they make network retries unnecessary.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Retries still occur; the key helps the server recognize duplicates.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Retries still occur; the key helps the server recognize duplicates.

answers.text: Short version: The server must store or derive the result associated with a key.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The server must store or derive the result associated with a key.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The server must store or derive the result associated with a key.

questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: what does a durable message queue help decouple?

answers.text: The practical answer is: it guarantees each message is processed exactly once without application logic.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Many queues use at-least-once delivery, so consumers should handle duplicates.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Many queues use at-least-once delivery, so consumers should handle duplicates.

answers.text: My answer: A queue always preserves one global message order across all consumers.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Ordering guarantees depend on the queue type, partitioning, and consumer design.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Ordering guarantees depend on the queue type, partitioning, and consumer design.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: It lets producers and consumers operate at different rates and tolerate each other's temporary outages.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It lets producers and consumers operate at different rates and tolerate each other's temporary outages.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It lets producers and consumers operate at different rates and tolerate each other's temporary outages.

questions.prompt: Tiny knowledge check: what is the purpose of a circuit breaker?

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, a half-open state can probe whether recovery has occurred.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A half-open state can probe whether recovery has occurred.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A half-open state can probe whether recovery has occurred.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Stop repeated calls to a failing dependency for a period.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Stop repeated calls to a failing dependency for a period.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Stop repeated calls to a failing dependency for a period.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Repair the dependency automatically.

feedback.legit: Not this one. It limits cascading damage but does not fix the underlying service.
feedback.sus: Good catch. It limits cascading damage but does not fix the underlying service.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what does rate limiting protect?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Capacity and fairness by restricting request volume over time.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Capacity and fairness by restricting request volume over time.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Capacity and fairness by restricting request volume over time.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: It guarantees low latency after every accepted request.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Accepted work can still be slow for other reasons.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Accepted work can still be slow for other reasons.

answers.text: From what I've seen, distributed rate limits require a strategy for shared state or approximation.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Distributed rate limits require a strategy for shared state or approximation.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Distributed rate limits require a strategy for shared state or approximation.

questions.prompt: No searching: why serve static assets through a CDN?

answers.text: This is what trips people up: A CDN makes cache invalidation unnecessary.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Changed content still needs versioning, expiry, or invalidation.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Changed content still needs versioning, expiry, or invalidation.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: A CDN safely caches every origin response by default.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Cacheability depends on method, headers, status, and CDN configuration; private responses need particular care.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Cacheability depends on method, headers, status, and CDN configuration; private responses need particular care.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Edge locations can reduce latency and origin traffic.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Edge locations can reduce latency and origin traffic.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Edge locations can reduce latency and origin traffic.

questions.prompt: Quick gut check: what choice does the CAP theorem highlight during a network partition?

answers.text: I'd go with this: Different operations can make different tradeoffs.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Different operations can make different tradeoffs.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Different operations can make different tradeoffs.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: a distributed system cannot guarantee both linearizable consistency and availability for every request while the partition persists.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A distributed system cannot guarantee both linearizable consistency and availability for every request while the partition persists.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A distributed system cannot guarantee both linearizable consistency and availability for every request while the partition persists.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, a system must permanently choose only two of three properties even without failures.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The tradeoff concerns behavior while a partition prevents communication.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The tradeoff concerns behavior while a partition prevents communication.

questions.prompt: Curious where people land on this: what is backpressure in a data-processing system?

answers.text: My answer: A way for slower consumers to limit or signal upstream production.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A way for slower consumers to limit or signal upstream production.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A way for slower consumers to limit or signal upstream production.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: A requirement to buffer unlimited work in memory.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Unlimited buffering delays failure and can exhaust resources.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Unlimited buffering delays failure and can exhaust resources.

answers.text: The way I understand it: dropping, batching, blocking, or shedding work are possible policies.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Dropping, batching, blocking, or shedding work are possible policies.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Dropping, batching, blocking, or shedding work are possible policies.

questions.prompt: Curious where people land on this: what does a saga coordinate?

answers.text: My answer: A single ACID transaction that locks every service database.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Sagas avoid one global database transaction across services.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Sagas avoid one global database transaction across services.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: A compensating action automatically rewinds database history as if the earlier step never happened.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Compensation is new business logic and may not perfectly reverse every prior effect.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Compensation is new business logic and may not perfectly reverse every prior effect.

answers.text: The way I understand it: a saga guarantees globally serializable isolation across every participating service.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A saga coordinates local transactions but does not by itself provide one globally serializable transaction.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A saga coordinates local transactions but does not by itself provide one globally serializable transaction.
